danger_signals.visual_form: Animated adversaries on screen (distinct sprite patterns from player)
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate physical threat requiring combat response

danger_signals.visual_form: Vertical level layouts with enemies densifying toward exits
danger_signals.meaning: Escalating difficulty and danger intensity as player advances

danger_signals.visual_form: Larger, distinctly-colored enemy sprite at stage endpoint
danger_signals.meaning: Critical confrontation requiring mastery of combat mechanics

safety_signals.visual_form: Screen state transition, enemy disappearance, exit/goal reached
safety_signals.meaning: Safe completion of current challenge; progression unlocked

safety_signals.visual_form: Player character sprite at edges/corners of play space
safety_signals.meaning: Spatial distance from enemies reduces immediate threat

faction_indicators.color_identity: Distinct from enemy sprites; standard action game protagonist palette
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Not faction-based; enemies are generic adversaries

faction_indicators.color_identity: Contrasting sprite coloration (red/dark tones typical for NES era antagonists)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple enemy types with distinct sprite designs (guards, martial artists, boss character)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Multi-platform vertical level with enemies distributed across floors/platforms; animated player and enemy sprites in motion
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies patrol, attack, and respond to player proximity; player must navigate and engage

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Screen transition animation; enemy sprites vanish; new level loads with fresh enemy configurations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player gains access to next stage; difficulty ramps through stage-by-stage progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Large boss sprite positioned at stage apex (typically final floor); more elaborate animation than standard enemies
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss executes distinct attack patterns; defeat triggers stage clear and progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Likely screen-state change indicating loss condition (player sprite removal or dedicated game-over screen)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game halts progression; may trigger restart or continue sequence

environmental_storytelling.technique: Vertical level architecture as narrative space
environmental_storytelling.example: Multi-floor pagoda/building structure suggests player ascending toward objective (rescuing captive); each floor represents progress toward goal

environmental_storytelling.technique: Enemy placement density as difficulty/threat escalation
environmental_storytelling.example: Lower floors have sparse enemies; upper floors and final boss chamber show increased enemy concentration, visually communicating rising stakes
Boss character receives distinctive, larger sprite treatment distinguishing them from generic guards, signaling narrative importance

environmental_storytelling.technique: Regional title variation as world-state marker
environmental_storytelling.example: Spartan X (original) vs Kung Fu (re-release) reflects licensing/political change in game's real-world state; both versions visually identical internally but represent different world contexts

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Regular movement pattern across platforms; sprite cycles through walk/idle animations
Animation acceleration; directional sprite orientation toward player; attack initiation
Sprite removal or knockout animation; clearing screen space for player advancement
Complex movement patterns; multi-stage attack sequences; sustained sprite presence through multiple player interactions
Responsive sprite animation reflecting directional input; climbing animations on vertical traversal
Stance animation; attack sprite frames indicating punch/kick animations on enemy engagement

notes: Kung Fu (NES/Famicom) is a 1985 port of an arcade beat-em-up. The game employs classic action paradigm visual communication: enemy density and boss-stage structure create world-state progression. No explicit faction system exists—conflict is binary (player vs. generic enemies). The game's world-state is communicated primarily through vertical level progression and enemy configuration changes. Licensed title variation (Spartan X to Kung Fu) reflects external political/licensing changes but no in-game manifestation. Game uses NES-era sprite limitations effectively: distinct enemy types through sprite variation, boss differentiation through size/animation complexity. Environmental storytelling relies on level architecture metaphor (ascending pagoda/building toward rescue objective).
